1.1: Ruth is set in the period of the judges ('in the days when the judges ruled', Ruth 1:1) and is a counter-narrative to the chaos of that era: while Judges ends with 'everyone did what was right in his own eyes,' Ruth depicts a community where covenant loyalty (hesed) is practiced across ethnic lines and social classes. The book's theological center is the keyword hesed (steadfast love, covenant loyalty) — used three times (1:8; 2:20; 3:10). It serves as an introduction to the Davidic narrative: its genealogy (4:17-22) links it directly to 1 Samuel and the rise of David, making Ruth the backstory of the royal family. Matthew's genealogy (Matt 1:5) includes Ruth alongside Tamar, Rahab, and Bathsheba — four women with irregular stories through whom the messianic line runs.
1.2: The names carry theological weight: Elimelech (אֱלִימֶלֶךְ) means 'my God is king' — ironic in the age of Judges when there was no king; Naomi (נָעֳמִי) means 'pleasant/sweet,' which she will invert to Mara (v20); Mahlon (מַחְלוֹן) and Chilion (כִּלְיוֹן) likely derive from roots meaning 'sickness/weakness' and 'wasting/destruction' respectively — ominous names that foreshadow their early deaths. Ephrathites from Bethlehem (בֵּית לֶחֶם, 'house of bread') — Ephrathah is the ancestral clan name for the Bethlehem district (Gen 35:19; 48:7; Mic 5:2); the family is from Judah's heartland, the region that will eventually produce David and the messianic line. Migration to Moab during famine is archaeologically attested in this region; the Shephelah and Judean hills were vulnerable to drought cycles.
1.3: Elimelech's death leaves Naomi as a widow in the Iron Age Levant — a position of profound legal and economic vulnerability. Israelite law provided specific protections for widows (ʾalmānāh): gleaning rights (Deut 24:19-21), exemption from certain pledges (Deut 24:17), inclusion in tithes (Deut 26:12-13), and judicial protections (Exod 22:22-24). But enforcement depended on community goodwill; a widow in a foreign country (Moab) had no such protections at all. The narrative begins with a progressive stripping of Naomi's support system: husband, then (vv4-5) both sons. The sociological realism of the story's opening movements creates the desperate context in which hesed and the go'el institution will operate.
1.4: Mahlon's and Chilion's marriages to Moabite women are presented without explicit condemnation, though the tension with Deuteronomy 23:3 ('No Ammonite or Moabite may enter the assembly of YHWH...even to the tenth generation') is real. The prohibition in Deut 23:3 concerns membership in the covenant assembly, not marriage per se; but Israelite tradition (Ezra 9-10; Neh 13:23-25) later treated intermarriage with specifically named nations as a violation. The silence here suggests the narrator is not primarily focused on the legal question but on the theological one: what does it mean when a Moabite woman becomes fully loyal to YHWH and his people? Ruth's subsequent behavior answers the question.
1.5: The death of both sons leaves three women — two daughters-in-law and Naomi — in a state of triple bereavement. In the ANE world, a woman's identity and economic security were inseparable from male household membership (father, husband, or son). Naomi has lost all three ties. The legal dimension: without a male heir to inherit Elimelech's land in Bethlehem, the family's landholding was at risk of permanent alienation. The go'el institution (Lev 25:25) existed precisely for this situation — a near kinsman had the right (and in some circumstances the obligation) to redeem alienated family land and to perpetuate the family name. The entire plot of Ruth turns on whether a go'el will act.
1.6: The report that 'YHWH had visited his people to give them food' uses the verb pāqaḏ (H6485) — 'to visit, attend to, care for.' The same verb appears in YHWH's promise to Moses: 'I have surely visited you' (Exod 3:16) and in Jacob's dying words: 'God will surely visit you' (Gen 50:24-25). The pāqaḏ formula in Scripture signals a turning point in YHWH's providential engagement with his people — an active divine intervention after a period of apparent absence. Naomi hears about this divine visitation while in Moab; the report becomes the occasion of return. The famine and its lifting are both YHWH's sovereign acts (cf. Amos 4:6-8 on famine as divine discipline).
1.7: The geographical route from Moab to Bethlehem crosses significant terrain: from the Moabite plateau (east of the Dead Sea), down through the Rift Valley, across the Jordan ford near Jericho, and up through the Judean hill country to Bethlehem — a journey of perhaps 50-65 miles with a total elevation change of over 4,000 feet (descending to the Jordan Valley at -1,300 feet below sea level, then ascending to Bethlehem at +2,500 feet). The journey would take several days on foot and was not trivial. The Jordan crossing is also symbolically charged: the entry point into the Promised Land (Josh 3-4), and the route Ruth travels is the reversal of Abraham's journey outward — a Gentile woman entering the land of covenant promise.
1.8: Naomi's blessing invokes hesed (H2617) — the book's governing keyword. Hesed is often translated 'steadfast love,' 'lovingkindness,' or 'covenant loyalty,' but none fully captures it: it is the loyalty, generosity, and faithfulness that characterizes covenant relationships (between humans and YHWH; between covenant partners). Naomi wishes for Orpah and Ruth the same hesed they showed to 'the dead and to me' — their care for their dead husbands and for her is explicitly characterized as hesed. The term recurs at 2:20 (Boaz's act is hesed to the living and the dead) and 3:10 (Ruth's pursuit of Boaz rather than young men is her second act of hesed). The book is fundamentally about hesed in action.
1.9: Mānôaḥ (H4494, 'resting place' / settled security) is the legal-social concept of a woman finding a safe and stable home within a male-headed household. Naomi had prayed for this for both daughters-in-law (v9); later she will arrange it for Ruth (3:1). The term reflects the real vulnerability of unattached women in the Iron Age: economic security, social status, and physical safety all depended on having a place in a functioning household. Mānôaḥ is not merely emotional peace but structural social security — a home, provision, and legal protection. Naomi's inability to provide this for Orpah and Ruth (because she has no sons to offer as husbands) drives the entire plot.
1.10: Orpah and Ruth's initial response — 'No, we will return with you to your people' — demonstrates genuine hesed. Their willingness to leave their homeland and follow an impoverished old woman to a foreign country is remarkable and is later recognized as such (v8: Naomi blesses them for the hesed they have already shown). The daughters-in-law are not depicted as cowardly or faithless; Orpah makes the reasonable choice Naomi herself recommends. The contrast between Orpah and Ruth is not a contrast between faithfulness and faithlessness but between two kinds of faithfulness: Orpah follows her reasonable obligations, Ruth exceeds them entirely.
1.11: Naomi's argument is drawn from the levirate marriage principle (Deut 25:5-10): when a married man dies childless, his brother is obligated to marry the widow and raise up children in the dead brother's name. Naomi inverts the institution: 'If I had a husband tonight and bore sons, would you wait for them?' The argument is deliberately absurd (she is too old) to demonstrate that the levirate path is closed to her daughters-in-law. The same levirate logic underlies the Sadducees' question to Jesus in Matt 22:23-33 — they use it to argue against resurrection. Jesus's answer that the resurrection age transcends marriage categories is partly an implicit commentary on the temporary nature of the entire levirate system.
1.12: The hypothetical 'if I have a husband tonight' uses the legal language of concubinage and rapid remarriage that was not uncommon in the ancient Near East. Naomi's argument demonstrates familiarity with the full range of legal mechanisms available to widows; she is not ignorant of her options, she is genuinely without them. The go'el option (which ultimately applies) is not mentioned here — perhaps because Naomi does not know at this point whether any near kinsman remains in Bethlehem, or because she does not think it applies to daughters-in-law rather than blood relatives. The plot's later revelation of Boaz will come as a surprise to her too.
1.13: 'The hand of YHWH has gone out against me' — Naomi's frank theodicy. The idiom of YHWH's 'hand' going against someone is used throughout the OT for divine judgment or affliction (1 Sam 5:9; Job 2:10; Ps 32:4). Naomi does not attribute her suffering to fate, accident, or her own fault; she attributes it directly to YHWH — which is, paradoxically, more theologically coherent than evasion would be. The same frank attribution characterizes Job: 'YHWH gave, and YHWH has taken away; blessed be the name of YHWH' (Job 1:21). The willingness to name YHWH as the author of suffering, rather than seeking a more comfortable explanation, preserves the integrity of faith in a sovereign God while demanding that the faith be faith in that God's ultimate goodness.
1.14: The text notes both women wept — Orpah's departure is not cold or calculating but genuinely painful. The contrast between 'Orpah kissed her mother-in-law' and 'Ruth clung to her' (using dāḇaq H1692 — the same verb as Gen 2:24: 'a man shall cleave to his wife'; 2 Kgs 18:6: Hezekiah 'clung to YHWH') is stark. Dāḇaq connotes a deep, volitional attachment that overcomes all separation forces. Ruth's clinging to Naomi is the covenant-loyalty act that the rest of the book will validate and reward. Orpah's choice is portrayed as understandable; Ruth's is portrayed as exceeding what was asked or required — and this excess is the book's model of hesed.
1.15: Naomi's urging Ruth to 'return after your sister-in-law to her people and to her gods' is religiously frank: the Moabite national deity was Chemosh (Num 21:29; 1 Kgs 11:7; Jer 48:46). The appeal to 'her gods' implicitly names the religious dimension of Orpah's return: to go back to Moab is to go back to the Chemosh cult, to be embedded again in a non-Yahwistic religious community. Ruth's decision to follow Naomi is therefore not merely ethnic but religious — she is choosing YHWH over Chemosh. The contrast between the two gods is part of what Ruth's declaration in v16-17 will make explicit.
1.16: Ruth's covenant declaration (vv16-17) is one of the most celebrated passages in the Hebrew Bible: 'Where you go I will go, and where you stay I will stay. Your people shall be my people, and your God my God. Where you die I will die, and there I will be buried. May YHWH deal with me, be it ever so severely, if even death separates you and me.' The declaration operates on multiple levels simultaneously: social (joining Naomi's community), ethnic (becoming part of Israel), religious (adopting YHWH as her God), and legal (invoking YHWH's name in a binding oath). The legal oath formula 'thus may YHWH do to me and thus may he add' (v17) places Ruth under the sanctions of covenant obligation — she is not making a sentimental promise but a binding legal commitment in YHWH's name.
1.17: The invocation of YHWH by name in the oath — 'YHWH do so to me and more also' — is the standard self-imprecation formula used in binding oaths throughout the historical books (1 Sam 3:17; 14:44; 2 Sam 3:9,35; 1 Kgs 2:23). By invoking YHWH's name, Ruth places herself under YHWH's covenant jurisdiction — she is effectively enrolling as a convert to Yahwism, not merely attaching herself to Naomi. The rabbinic tradition later develops a formal conversion process partly on the basis of Ruth's declaration; it becomes the paradigmatic model of what proselyte conversion entails. Ruth's joining of the covenant community is the OT's clearest narrative model of voluntary Gentile adoption into Israel.
1.18: Naomi's silence when she saw Ruth's resolve (miṣṣaddāh lāleḵet ʾittāh — that she was determined to go with her) is a significant narrative detail. She says nothing more. The silence is not disapproval; it is recognition. A resolution expressed with this level of commitment — a legal oath, a covenant declaration — cannot and should not be argued against. The narrator uses Naomi's silence to signal that Ruth's commitment has the quality of finality. In a culture where verbal exchange is the primary medium of social relation, choosing not to speak a further word of dissuasion is itself a speech act: an acknowledgment that the matter is settled.
1.19: Bethlehem's response — 'Was the whole city moved/stirred because of them? Is this Naomi?' — uses the verb hōm (H1949: 'to be in commotion, to be in an uproar') which appears elsewhere for the excitement at the arrival of the ark (1 Sam 4:5) and the commotion of an army camp (1 Sam 14:15). The village's stir is both social curiosity (a woman returning from long absence with a foreign daughter-in-law) and implicit recognition that something significant has happened. Small Bronze Age and Iron Age villages (Bethlehem was not large) would have known the Elimelech family well; Naomi's return with Ruth but without husband and sons would have been immediately notable.
1.20: The name-change from Naomi ('pleasant/sweet') to Mara ('bitter') reflects the ANE practice of names as theological commentary on one's condition. Hebrew naming throughout the OT encodes both character and circumstance: Naomi's self-renaming is an act of honest self-interpretation. 'I went out full and YHWH brought me back empty' — ʾel šadday (God Almighty) is the patriarchal name for God (Gen 17:1; 28:3; 35:11) and carries connotations of covenant-making power. Naomi's use of the name is significant: she is appealing to the God who makes and keeps covenant promises — even as she confesses that he has dealt bitterly with her. This is not faithlessness but honest covenant complaint (the pattern of lament psalms).
1.21: 'I went out full (mĕlēʾāh) and YHWH has brought me back empty (rêqāh)' — the contrast of fullness and emptiness maps onto the agricultural metaphors that will dominate the rest of the book. The harvest season (v22) will reverse the emptiness: Ruth will return from the fields full (2:17-18), Naomi will eat Boaz's grain (3:15-17), and the book's final image is Naomi nursing Obed, her 'fullness' restored. The reversal is YHWH's answer to the complaint. The verb 'testified against me' (ʿānāh bî) — as if YHWH had appeared as a legal witness against her — is rare and striking; Naomi's language pushes toward the edge of covenant lawsuit rhetoric.
1.22: The closing of chapter 1 is precisely dated by the harvest calendar: 'at the beginning of the barley harvest' — late March to early April in the Bethlehem area (slightly later than the Jordan Valley). The agricultural calendar of ancient Israel ran: barley harvest (Passover season, Nisan/April) → wheat harvest (Pentecost/Shavuot, Sivan/May-June) → summer figs and grapes (Tammuz-Av/July-August) → olives (Tishri/September-October). Chapter 2's gleaning takes place in the barley harvest; chapter 3's threshing floor scene falls at the end of the barley or wheat harvest; the legal transaction of chapter 4 follows shortly after. The entire story unfolds in the span of approximately two months.

2.1: Boaz is introduced with the phrase ʾîš gibbôr ḥayil — 'a man of great standing/valor/substance.' Gibbôr ḥayil is a military-cultural designation used for elite warriors (1 Sam 9:1; 2 Kgs 15:20) but also for men of property, influence, and social standing. The same adjective root is applied to Ruth herself in 3:11 ('woman of ḥayil') — the pairing is significant: they are matched in character and quality. Boaz's name may derive from the same root ('in him is strength') or may be a place name. Matthew 1:5 names his mother as Rahab the Canaanite — if this is accurate, Boaz himself is the son of a Gentile convert woman, making his recognition of Ruth's faithfulness (2:11-12) personally resonant.
2.2: Ruth's proposal to glean invokes the Deuteronomic-Levitical gleaning laws: Lev 19:9-10 ('When you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not reap your field right up to its edge... you shall leave them for the poor and for the sojourner'), Deut 24:19-21. The provision specifically covered three categories: the poor (ʿānî), the sojourner (gēr), the orphan (yātôm), and the widow (ʾalmānāh) — Ruth qualifies on at least three counts (poor, sojourner, widow). The practice was regulated but dependent on landowner compliance; a hostile landowner could make gleaning difficult or dangerous. Ruth's request for permission ('in whose eyes I find favor') reflects the practical reality that she needed the goodwill of the field's owner.
2.3: The narrator's phrase miqrehāh qārāh — usually translated 'as it happened' or 'her luck came upon her' — uses the word miqreh (H4745), which means 'chance occurrence,' 'what happens by lot,' or 'lot/fate.' The word appears in Ecclesiastes for the fate that comes equally to all (Eccl 2:14-15; 9:11). Here the narrator is being simultaneously realistic (she didn't know whose field it was) and theologically ironic (the reader can see YHWH's guiding hand). This 'coincidence' places Ruth in Boaz's field rather than another owner's — an 'accident' that will resolve the entire crisis. The narrative uses the language of chance to describe what is, from the reader's perspective, providential appointment.
2.4: Boaz's greeting 'YHWH be with you' (YHWH ʿimmākem) and the workers' response 'YHWH bless you' (yĕbārekeḵā YHWH) are covenant-embedded agricultural greetings. The presence of divine-name invocations in everyday work speech reflects the covenant community's integration of faith and daily life — YHWH's name belongs in the fields, not only in the sanctuary. The contrast with the Canaanite agricultural world (where Baal's blessing was invoked for crops) is implicit: Boaz's workers invoke YHWH's blessing over their labor. Ps 129:8 preserves a similar formula as a standard blessing for harvest workers, suggesting these were conventional greetings in Israelite agricultural communities.
2.5: Boaz's immediate question about Ruth ('Whose young woman is this?') demonstrates his attentiveness to his workers. The foreman's answer identifies Ruth by three markers: she is a Moabite woman, she returned with Naomi from Moab, and she asked permission before beginning to glean. The question and answer establish that Boaz has not met Ruth before — making his subsequent generosity an act of pure hesed based on reported character, not personal acquaintance. In a small-town (Bethlehem had perhaps 300-500 people at this period) this kind of report would travel fast; Boaz likely already knew about Ruth's arrival with Naomi (4:1-2 suggests the whole town knew).
2.6: The foreman identifies Ruth as 'the Moabite young woman who came back with Naomi from the country of Moab.' The identification is not pejorative but factual; it establishes Ruth's status as a gēr (resident alien) with the attendant legal protections and social distinctiveness. The foreman's subsequent report (v7) is favorable to Ruth — she asked properly, she worked hard. The gleaning system depended on both legal framework and social enforcement; a dishonest gleaner could take more than the prescribed portion, and a hostile foreman could make the work miserable. The foreman's positive portrayal of Ruth suggests she has already made a good impression on Boaz's workers.
2.7: Ruth's request to 'gather among the sheaves after the reapers' is more generous access than the law strictly required — gleaning was typically from what fell at the field edges, not from among the sheaves already gathered. Her asking ('please let me glean and gather among the sheaves') suggests she is requesting something slightly beyond the standard entitlement, and the foreman granted it. The note about the shelter (bêt here meaning perhaps a field shelter or booth for resting) and her brief rest demonstrates she worked continuously. The resting in the shelter would be in the heat of midday — a standard practice in the Levantine agricultural calendar when field labor paused during the hottest hours (approximately 11am-2pm).
2.8: Boaz's instruction 'Do not go to glean in another field or leave this one, but keep close to my young women' is protective as well as generous. The gleaning fields were not without risk for unaccompanied women — Boaz will explicitly warn his young men 'not to touch her' (v9, 15). The presence of foreign or unattached women in harvest fields could attract unwanted attention; Boaz's instruction to remain in his field and close to his female workers provides social protection through proximity and patronage. The protection of the vulnerable foreign woman through the social mechanisms of honor and patron-client relationships reflects actual Iron Age social practice.
2.9: Boaz tells Ruth to drink from the water that his young men have drawn — an act of genuine hospitality that reverses normal social expectations. In ancient agricultural settings, the lowest-status workers (often including women and foreigners) did not have access to the provisions of higher-status workers; water-drawing was itself a task assigned to low-status members of the household (Josh 9:21). Boaz gives Ruth access to water his young men drew — inverting the labor hierarchy. The same generosity appears in v14's meal invitation. ANE hospitality codes required care for guests; Boaz extends these codes to a foreign worker in his field who has no formal claim on his hospitality.
2.10: Ruth's response — 'Why have I found favor (ḥēn) in your eyes, that you should take notice of me, since I am a foreigner (nokrîyāh)?' — uses the stronger of the two Hebrew words for foreigner. Gēr (sojourner/resident alien) had legal protections in Israel; nokrî/nokrîyāh (foreigner/alien woman) was more ambiguous, sometimes implying someone outside the covenant community's obligations. Ruth uses the stronger, less favorable term for herself — emphasizing that Boaz's generosity has no legal or social obligation behind it. Her self-description as outside the patronage system she is benefiting from highlights the sheer grace of what Boaz is doing. The contrast is the setup for Boaz's explanation in v11-12.
2.11: Boaz's knowledge of Ruth's story demonstrates that information traveled through Bethlehem's small community: 'All that you have done for your mother-in-law since the death of your husband has been fully told to me.' The specific details he knows — how she left her father and mother and her homeland — echo the language of Abraham's call (Gen 12:1: 'go from your country and your kindred and your father's house'). Ruth's voluntary departure from her Moabite homeland parallels Abraham's departure from Mesopotamia; the narrator is establishing Ruth as a second Abraham — a Gentile called out of her land to follow YHWH. Boaz's recognition of this pattern in v12's blessing confirms that the parallel is intentional.
2.12: Boaz's prayer-blessing invokes YHWH as the one under whose 'wings' (kānāp̄ H3671) Ruth has come to take refuge. The same word kānāp̄ appears in Ruth 3:9 when Ruth asks Boaz to 'spread your wing over me' — creating an explicit link between YHWH's protection and Boaz's go'el action. The image of YHWH's protective wings recurs throughout the Psalms (17:8; 36:7; 57:1; 61:4; 63:7; 91:4) and in Jesus's lament over Jerusalem (Matt 23:37). Boaz's prayer points to YHWH as the ultimate source of Ruth's refuge; his own subsequent action will be the concrete means by which YHWH answers his own prayer. The blessing and the blessing's fulfillment will be accomplished through the same person.
2.13: Ruth's response — 'I have found favor (ḥēn) in your eyes, my lord, for you have comforted me and spoken kindly to your servant' — uses the vocabulary of grace and consolation. Ḥēn (favor/grace) is the gift of a superior to an inferior who has no claim on it; Ruth recognizes that Boaz's generosity is pure grace, not obligation. 'Spoken to the heart' (dibbartā ʿal-lēḇ) is the idiom for deeply comforting speech (Gen 34:3; Isa 40:2; Hos 2:14), used elsewhere for the speech that restores the despairing. Ruth's self-description as 'your servant' (šipḥāh) — the lowest female domestic rank — acknowledges the social distance while accepting the relationship Boaz has extended.
2.14: The meal invitation — 'Come here and eat some bread and dip your morsel in the wine' — is an act of genuine table fellowship. Ḥōmeṣ (translated 'wine' here) may refer to a vinegar-based dip or sour wine used as a condiment, common in harvest field meals. Boaz's serving Ruth himself ('he passed to her roasted grain') and her eating 'until she was satisfied and had some left over' evokes the abundance of divine provision — the same pattern as Elijah's jar of flour and cruse of oil that did not fail (1 Kgs 17:14-16) and Jesus's feeding of the multitude. The 'and she had some left over' is a small detail with large theological resonance: YHWH's provision through the hands of the hesed-actor exceeds the immediate need.
2.15: Boaz's private instruction to his workers — 'let her glean even among the sheaves' (not just the field edges) and 'pull out some stalks and leave them for her to glean, and do not rebuke her' — represents intentional, covert generosity. He is ensuring that Ruth's gleaning yields more than the legal minimum, but doing it in a way that preserves her dignity (she does not know it is staged provision). This combination of substantial help and dignity-preserving method reflects the highest form of ANE charity: making the provision look like natural good fortune rather than embarrassing charity. The Deuteronomic gleaning laws were a minimum floor; Boaz is going far beyond them while protecting Ruth's sense of having worked for what she received.
2.16: The instruction to 'pull out from the bundles' is a technical agricultural term — the reapers were to deliberately drop stalks from the already-gathered sheaves, ensuring Ruth's gleaning basket would be full. The practice of provisioning the poor through harvest abundance — ensuring the margins of the field were genuinely productive, not stripped — was a form of distribution built into the agricultural economy. The gleaning laws functioned as a kind of tax on the harvest in favor of the vulnerable. Boaz's exceeding of this minimum reflects the spirit-of-the-law interpretation that Jesus will later articulate as the goal of all law: love for neighbor that exceeds formal obligation.
2.17: An ephah (H374) of barley is approximately 22 kg (about 48 lbs) or roughly 29 liters — a substantial quantity for a single day's gleaning. The legal gleaning provision was meant to provide subsistence; an ephah far exceeds what a gleaner could normally expect. The quantity signals Boaz's secret generosity (vv15-16) has been effective. Naomi's amazement in v19 — 'Where did you glean today?' — is precisely this response: the amount is so large it requires an explanation. The ephah becomes the visible sign that YHWH is at work through a specific human agent, pointing Naomi toward the revelation of Boaz's name in v20.
2.18: Ruth carries the gleaned barley back to town and shows Naomi 'what she had gleaned' as well as 'what she had left over' from the meal (v14). The double provision — the gleaned grain and the leftover food — sets up Naomi's series of questions. The movement from the field to the town in the evening mirrors the daily rhythm of agricultural labor: work in the fields during the day, return to the settled community at night. The provision Ruth brings Naomi is substantial enough that Naomi can immediately see something unusual has happened. In a subsistence economy, an ephah of barley arriving in a widow's home was a significant event.
2.19: Naomi's three-part response to Ruth's account — 'Where did you glean today?', 'Where did you work?', and 'May he who took notice of you be blessed!' — demonstrates shrewd social reading. She immediately understands that the quantity of grain and the leftovers indicate specific, intentional favor from a named person; the provision was not random. Her blessing of 'the man who took notice of you' is invoked before she knows who it is — she blesses YHWH's agent before identifying him, which reflects the theological principle that the blessing belongs first to YHWH who sent the agent. The revelation of Boaz's name in v20 then allows the blessing to be personal and specific.
2.20: Naomi's exclamation on hearing Boaz's name — 'He has not abandoned his hesed to the living and to the dead' — identifies YHWH as the ultimate actor of hesed, acting through Boaz. The phrase 'to the living and to the dead' is legally significant: the go'el obligation includes perpetuating the dead man's name and inheritance (Deut 25:6-7; cf. 4:10). Naomi's declaration that Boaz is 'our close relative, one of our go'ēlîm' is the legal identification that frames the entire rest of the book: the go'el institution (kinsman-redeemer) is the legal mechanism through which the reversal of Naomi's condition will be accomplished. See Lev 25:25, 47-55 for the full legal framework.
2.21: Ruth reports Boaz's instruction to stay close to his workers until the end of all his harvest — both barley (April-May) and wheat (May-June). The invitation to remain throughout the entire harvest season provides Ruth with approximately two months of protected gleaning with exceptional yields. In practical terms, two months of gleaning at Boaz's rate would provide Naomi and Ruth with substantial food reserves. The arrangement is also social: Ruth's repeated presence in Boaz's field, under his protection and known to his workers, establishes her reputation in Bethlehem. By harvest's end, everyone in the town will know Ruth the Moabite as a woman of ḥayil (3:11: 'All my townspeople know you are a woman of valor').
2.22: Naomi's instruction 'go out with his young women' — not the young men — is a prudent social caution. Young unattached women working in harvest fields were at some risk from young male workers; staying close to Boaz's female workers (and under his protection) minimized that risk. The practice of women working together in gender-grouped agricultural tasks is attested in the archaeological record of Iron Age Israelite villages. The organization of labor by gender and social status reflects the community structures of small Iron Age villages where social roles were well-defined and departure from convention could create friction or danger.
2.23: Ruth gleans 'until the end of the barley and wheat harvests' — approximately April through June — then stays with Naomi. The gap between the wheat harvest and the next agricultural event (the threshing, ch3) would have been a few weeks. The barley harvest was associated with Passover (Lev 23:10-14); the wheat harvest with Pentecost/Shavuot (Lev 23:15-21). The entire story of Ruth takes place within the liturgical calendar of Israel's major spring feasts — a detail that links Ruth's gleaning and Boaz's redemption to the covenant community's celebration of YHWH's provision.

3.1: Naomi's initiative — 'Should I not seek a mānôaḥ (resting place/security) for you, that it may be well with you?' — echoes her earlier blessing over Orpah and Ruth at departure (1:9: 'May YHWH grant that you find mānôaḥ'). The echo is deliberate: Naomi is now attempting to be the instrument of the mānôaḥ she had prayed YHWH would provide. The mother-in-law's obligation in an ANE household was precisely this: to use her social knowledge and connections to arrange advantageous matches for daughters-in-law. Naomi's scheme is not immoral but culturally conventional — she is fulfilling her household obligations through the mechanisms available to her.
3.2: The threshing floor (gōren H1637) was typically located on an elevated, exposed hillside where the prevailing westerly wind could separate grain from chaff. They were communal facilities — several families might share a threshing floor, or a large landowner like Boaz might maintain his own. Threshing occurred after harvest (typically May-June for wheat); the winnowing process of tossing grain in the air to let the chaff blow away required the late-afternoon breeze. Boaz's 'winnowing barley on the threshing floor tonight' sets the scene: he will eat (the harvest feast) and sleep on the threshing floor to protect the grain — a practice attested in ANE agriculture to prevent theft from the stored grain pile.
3.3: Naomi's instructions — 'wash yourself, put on fragrant oil, dress yourself in your best garments' — are preparations for a significant social encounter. Fragrant oil (šemen H8081) was used on festive occasions; its absence was a sign of mourning (2 Sam 14:2; Dan 10:3). Ruth's adoption of festive dress after the period of widowhood signals a transition in her social status — she is no longer performing mourning. The instruction 'do not make yourself known to the man until he has finished eating and drinking' reflects the social wisdom that a well-fed, satisfied man is more receptive to a significant request than a hungry one. The practical social psychology embedded in Naomi's instructions reflects real knowledge of how to navigate an ANE social encounter.
3.4: The instruction to uncover his feet (margelôtāyw, H4772 — a term used specifically for the area at someone's feet while sleeping) and lie down is debated by commentators. Some read margelôt as a euphemism; others understand it literally as exposing his feet to the night air to wake him. The most natural reading in context is that Ruth uncovers his feet to signal her presence at his feet in a position of petition and request. The foot/lower extremity as the position of a supplicant is culturally attested (the woman who anointed Jesus's feet, Luke 7:38; Mary sitting at Jesus's feet, Luke 10:39). Ruth positions herself as a petitioner seeking a legal act from Boaz, not merely a social visit.
3.5: 'All that you tell me I will do' — Ruth's complete compliance with Naomi's plan mirrors her earlier covenant declaration: 'where you go I will go' (1:16-17). Ruth's obedience throughout the book is total and immediate; she does not debate, qualify, or second-guess the instructions she is given. This quality of obedient trust — in Naomi in ch1-3, and implicitly in YHWH behind Naomi's instructions — is the behavioral content of hesed. The narrative presents Ruth's obedience not as passivity but as active covenant loyalty expressed through the medium of following trustworthy guidance.
3.6: The threshing floor scene at night creates dramatic intensity. Boaz's eating, drinking, and lying down at the end of the grain heap is a naturalistic detail of harvest celebration: the threshing floor feast (a form of first-fruits celebration) was a moment of communal gratitude and abundance, typically involving communal eating and the sleeping of the workers on-site to guard the grain overnight. The scene is set at night, in the open air, at a time of seasonal celebration — a context that makes the encounter intimate and unusual without being necessarily scandalous.
3.7: Boaz 'was in good spirits' (wayyîṭaḇ libbô) — a phrase used elsewhere for the pleasant mood following a feast (Judg 16:25 of Samson; 1 Sam 25:36 of Nabal). The combination of harvest completion, communal celebration, and satisfied rest creates the particular moment Naomi has calculated for Ruth's approach. The detail that Boaz lay down 'at the end of the heap of grain' is significant: he is positioned to protect the stored grain, a task important enough that he sleeps beside it. Ruth's approach to him there is not an intrusion on a private sleeping chamber but a visible public space in the context of legitimate harvest guardianship.
3.8: 'At midnight the man was startled and turned over' — the dramatic midnight revelation. The hour of midnight in OT narrative is associated with decisive divine action: the exodus death of the firstborn (Exod 12:29), Samuel's night call (1 Sam 3:3ff), and here YHWH's providential arrangement reaching its human climax at midnight. Boaz's discovery of a woman at his feet in the dark is arresting — he does not immediately know who she is (v9: 'Who are you?'), which means the encounter is genuinely surprising to him, not a pre-arranged liaison.
3.9: Ruth's request — 'Spread your wing (kānāp̄) over your servant, for you are a redeemer (go'el)' — is the book's legal-theological climax. Kānāp̄ (H3671, wing/corner of a garment) is the same word Boaz used in his blessing in 2:12 ('under whose wings you have come to take refuge') — Ruth is asking Boaz to be the answer to his own prayer. The spreading of one's kānāp̄ over a woman was a legal act of claiming her as a wife (Ezek 16:8: YHWH spreads his kānāp̄ over Israel; Deut 23:1; 27:20). Ruth's request is simultaneously a marriage proposal and a legal go'el petition: she is asking Boaz to exercise his kinsman-redeemer right to marry her and perpetuate the dead's name.
3.10: Boaz's response — 'May you be blessed by YHWH, my daughter. You have made this last kindness greater than the first' — explicitly calls Ruth's action hesed and identifies two acts of hesed: the first (leaving Moab and caring for Naomi) and the second (this approach to Boaz rather than pursuing younger men). The 'young men, whether poor or rich' reflects the social reality that Ruth had options; her choice of the go'el route rather than a young husband reflects hesed toward the dead (perpetuating the family name) over personal advantage. Boaz's praise encodes the book's theological insight: hesed is love that costs something and benefits those who may never repay it.
3.11: 'All my townspeople know that you are a woman of valor (ʾēšet ḥayil)' — the same ḥayil used for Boaz in 2:1. The public reputation Ruth has built over two months of harvest gleaning — her diligence, her care for Naomi, her integrity — is now the foundation for Boaz's assurance. Proverbs 31:10 opens with ʾēšet ḥayil mî yimṣāʾ ('a woman of valor, who can find?'), the same rare idiom; the Proverbs 31 portrait is often read as the idealized Israelite woman, and Ruth is its narrative embodiment. The fact that 'all the gate of my people knows' indicates that Ruth's reputation has penetrated the town's official legal-social space — the gate where elders deliberate (ch4).
3.12: Boaz's disclosure — 'there is a redeemer nearer than I' — introduces the legal complication that will require resolution in ch4. The go'el institution operated by proximity of kinship: the nearest male kinsman had first right and obligation. Boaz's acknowledgment that a closer kinsman exists shows his legal integrity — he will not circumvent the other man's prior right, even though he desires to act. The existence of a nearer go'el is a narrative problem that ch4 must solve, and it also demonstrates that YHWH's providential arrangement requires proper legal process rather than shortcuts.
3.13: Boaz's commitment — 'As YHWH lives, I will redeem you' — invokes the divine name in a binding oath (the same formula as 1 Sam 14:45; 20:21; 2 Sam 15:21). The condition 'if he will redeem you, good; let him redeem. But if he is not willing to redeem you, then, as YHWH lives, I will redeem you' is legally precise: Boaz will give the nearer go'el his legal opportunity first, and if he refuses, Boaz will act. The commitment is unconditional within those legal parameters. 'Lie down until morning' — the instruction to remain for the night rather than making a furtive departure in the dark is an act of protective hospitality: leaving before dawn in the dark could expose Ruth to danger on the road.
3.14: Ruth's pre-dawn departure is timed to preserve social propriety: 'She rose before one could recognize another' (the phrase indicates it was dark enough to prevent identification). Boaz's instruction 'Let it not be known that the woman came to the threshing floor' is social protection for Ruth's reputation — the encounter, while legally and morally legitimate, could be misread if publicly known before the gate proceedings of ch4. The six measures of barley Boaz gives her serve as both practical provision and a sign to Naomi (v15-17: Naomi asks 'how did you fare?' and the grain tells the story before Ruth speaks).
3.15: Six measures of barley (the unit is likely seʾāh, about 8 liters each = ~48 liters total, roughly 37 kg) is a substantial load — almost too heavy for one person to carry comfortably. The quantity signals that Boaz is sending a message to Naomi as much as providing for Ruth. 'He went into the city' — the note that Boaz immediately returned to the town (not staying at the threshing floor) suggests he has a plan he is ready to execute. The threshing floor encounter has moved both characters to action: Ruth returns to Naomi, Boaz moves to the gate.
3.16: Naomi's question — 'How did you fare, my daughter?' (literally: 'Who are you, my daughter?' — an idiom asking about her status/condition) — gets the answer through Ruth's telling of the story and through the six measures of barley as physical evidence. The grain speaks: Boaz has sent a practical signal that the matter is not finished and that provision continues. Naomi's reading is exact (v17: 'He gave me these six measures of barley, saying “Do not go back empty-handed to your mother-in-law”') — she correctly interprets the grain as a message.
3.17: 'Do not go back empty-handed to your mother-in-law' — Boaz's instruction to Ruth echoes Naomi's own complaint in 1:21 ('I went out full and YHWH brought me back empty'). Boaz's provision is YHWH's answer to Naomi's emptiness: the grain is the beginning of the reversal. The word 'empty' (rêqāh) used in 1:21 and here connects the two scenes deliberately — the narrator wants the reader to see the provision as the start of YHWH's answer to Naomi's lament. YHWH does not answer the lament with an explanation but with grain, and through grain with a redeemer.
3.18: Naomi's instruction — 'Sit still, my daughter, until you learn how the matter will fall, for the man will not rest but will settle the matter today' — is a word of faith: she trusts that Boaz will act quickly. 'Will not rest' (yišqōṭ, the same root as the book's key word mānôaḥ — rest/settle) until he settles the matter uses the rest vocabulary in reverse: Boaz will not be at rest until he has provided rest for Ruth. The mother-in-law's confident reading of Boaz's character — that the six measures of barley and the sworn commitment mean immediate action — reflects her knowledge both of Boaz and of what YHWH is doing through him.

4.1: The town gate (šaʿar H8179) was the primary civic space in an ancient Israelite city — the place where elders sat to adjudicate legal disputes (Deut 21:19; 22:15; Amos 5:10-12), where commercial transactions were witnessed (Gen 23:10-20; 2 Kgs 7:1), and where prophets addressed the community (Jer 17:19-20). Boaz's deliberate positioning at the gate to intercept the nearer go'el reflects sophisticated social strategy: he intends to resolve the legal matter immediately, publicly, and irrevocably, in the one venue where it will be permanently recorded in communal memory. The gate was both the town's legal courthouse and its record-keeping institution — there were no written contracts; the public witness of the elders was the legal record.
4.2: Boaz takes ten elders of the city as witnesses — a number that in later Jewish legal tradition (the minyan, the quorum of ten for formal prayer and legal proceedings) becomes standardized as the minimum for binding communal legal action. In Iron Age Israelite towns, the elders were the senior male heads of household who constituted the town's governing council and judicial body; their witness made a transaction legally binding and enforceable. The ten witnesses ensure that the transaction about to occur will be recognized by the entire community and cannot later be contested on grounds of insufficient witnesses.
4.3: Boaz frames the case around Naomi's land: 'Naomi, who has come back from the country of Moab, is selling (mākĕrāh) the piece of land that belonged to our relative Elimelech.' The sale or pledge of family land (ʾaḥuzzāh H272) was the legal trigger for the go'el's right of redemption (Lev 25:25: 'If your brother becomes poor and sells part of his property, then his nearest redeemer shall come and redeem what his brother has sold'). Whether Naomi is actually selling the land or offering it for redemption is debated; the Hebrew can mean either an already-completed sale (she sold it during her absence in Moab) or a prospective sale. The legal mechanism is the same either way: the nearer kinsman has first right of redemption.
4.4: Boaz's formal presentation of the redemption right — 'I thought I would tell you of it and say, Buy it in the presence of those sitting here and in the presence of the elders of my people. If you will redeem it, redeem it. But if you will not, tell me, that I may know, for there is no one besides you to redeem it, and I come after you' — is a precise legal formula. The sequence mirrors the formal legal procedure: identify the right, present it to the nearest kinsman first, establish witness, and create a public record of acceptance or refusal. The nearer go'el's initial answer ('I will redeem it') is legally normal — land redemption was attractive, preserving the family patrimony and adding to one's own holdings.
4.5: Boaz's second disclosure changes everything: 'The day you buy the field from the hand of Naomi, you also acquire Ruth the Moabite, the widow of the dead, in order to perpetuate the name of the dead in his inheritance.' The linking of land redemption to levirate-style marriage obligation is not explicitly prescribed in the Pentateuch — Leviticus 25 covers land redemption; Deuteronomy 25 covers levirate marriage — but the custom of linking them in practice reflects the social reality that land and lineage were inseparable. A redeemed field would pass to the son born of the dead man's widow; the redeemer therefore could not simply acquire the land without the heir obligation that came with it. This complication changes the nearer go'el's calculation entirely.
4.6: The nearer go'el's refusal — 'I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I damage my own inheritance' — is legally coherent. If he marries Ruth and she bears a son, that son will inherit the redeemed Elimelech field; the nearer go'el would have paid the redemption price but the land would pass to a child who is legally Elimelech's heir, not his own. In practical terms, he would be paying for land that reverts to another family's lineage. His calculation is rational within the framework of the go'el institution — he is protecting his own children's inheritance by declining. His refusal, whatever its motivation, opens the way for Boaz, which is YHWH's providential arrangement working through ordinary human self-interest.
4.7: The narrator's parenthetical explanation of the sandal custom — 'This was the custom in former times in Israel concerning redeeming and exchanging: to confirm a transaction, the one drew off his sandal and gave it to the other, and this was the manner of attesting in Israel' — is significant because it identifies the custom as archaic even in the narrator's time. The sandal transaction here differs from the Deuteronomy 25:9 levirate-refusal ceremony (where the widow pulls off the shoe of the refusing brother-in-law in public shame). Here the sandal exchange is an agreed transaction between two consenting parties — a symbolic transfer of legal right. The sandal may represent the 'right to walk on the land' — to own and use it — being transferred from one party to another.
4.8: 'Then the redeemer said to Boaz, Buy it for yourself.' So he drew off his sandal. The legal act is complete: in the presence of ten witnesses and all the gate assembly, the nearer go'el has formally transferred his redemption right to Boaz by handing over his sandal. This is a public, irrevocable, witnessed transaction — it cannot later be contested. The legal formalism of the scene — the gate, the ten elders, the formal speeches, the sandal exchange — reflects the elaborate oral-legal architecture of a society without written contracts. Memory of the gate assembly replaced the written document; the elders' witness was the legal record.
4.9: Boaz's formal declaration — 'You are witnesses today that I have bought from the hand of Naomi all that belonged to Elimelech and all that belonged to Chilion and to Mahlon' — is a complete legal formula invoking the assembly as witnesses. The triple naming (Elimelech, Chilion, Mahlon) ensures that every possible claim on the property is legally addressed; no unnamed heir or claimant can later arise. The formula 'you are witnesses today' appears in other OT legal contexts (Josh 24:22; 1 Sam 12:5) as the standard verbal ratification of a legally binding agreement before witnesses.
4.10: 'Also Ruth the Moabite, the widow of Mahlon, I have bought to be my wife, to perpetuate the name of the dead in his inheritance, that the name of the dead may not be cut off from among his brothers and from the gate of his native place. You are witnesses today.' Boaz's declaration accomplishes three things simultaneously: it claims Ruth as his wife, it identifies the purpose (perpetuating Mahlon's name), and it extends the legal witness to both transactions. The phrase 'cut off from his brothers and from the gate of his native place' reflects the ANE horror of familial extinction — dying without descendants who can maintain one's name was considered the worst fate. The go'el and levirate institutions existed specifically to prevent this extinction.
4.11: The communal blessing — 'May YHWH make the woman who is coming into your house like Rachel and Leah, who together built up the house of Israel' — invokes the matriarchs of the twelve tribes. Rachel and Leah are paired as the two mothers of all Israel; their cooperative 'building of the house' (through their sons and handmaids) was the origin of the twelve-tribe structure. The blessing for Ruth as a member of this foundational community of matriarchs places her alongside the most honored women of Israel's covenant history. The elders' recognition of Ruth as worthy of this blessing is the community's formal integration of the Moabite woman into the covenant people.
4.12: The blessing continues: 'and may your house be like the house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah, because of the offspring that YHWH will give you by this young woman.' The reference to Perez and Tamar is theologically charged: Tamar was a non-Israelite (probably Canaanite) widow who used an unconventional method (disguise and seduction) to secure her levirate right from Judah, and her twin sons Perez and Zerah became the foundation of the Judahite clan (Gen 38). The parallel to Ruth is exact: another non-Israelite widow securing her levirate right by unconventional means, producing offspring who will become the Davidic line. The elders' blessing invokes the very precedent that validates Ruth's action.
4.13: The marriage, union, and conception — 'YHWH gave her conception' — invokes divine agency explicitly. Barrenness in the Hebrew Bible is a theological condition as much as a biological one; YHWH opens and closes the womb (Gen 20:18; 29:31; 30:2; 1 Sam 1:5-6). Ruth's conception is attributed directly to YHWH's opening of her womb, not merely to the marriage. This is consistent with the book's theology throughout: the 'coincidences' that have brought Ruth to Boaz's field, then to his threshing floor, then to his household, are YHWH's providential acts executed through ordinary human hesed. The divine and human are interwoven throughout Ruth; neither operates without the other.
4.14: The women's blessing of Naomi — 'Blessed be YHWH, who has not left you this day without a redeemer, and may his name be renowned in Israel' — closes the theological arc that Naomi's lament opened in 1:20-21 ('Do not call me Naomi, call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me... YHWH has brought me back empty'). YHWH has not left Naomi without a go'el; the redeemer the book identified in chapter 2 (Boaz) has acted, and now the child Obed is the redeemer in the next generation. The women's blessing forms the human chorus that interprets YHWH's action — a function similar to the Psalms' commentary on YHWH's deeds in history.
4.15: 'He shall be to you a restorer of life (mēšîḇ nep̄eš) and a nourisher of your old age, for your daughter-in-law who loves you, who is more to you than seven sons, has given birth to him.' The phrase mēšîḇ nep̄eš (restorer of nepeš, the vital self/life force) is used elsewhere for YHWH's restorative action (Ps 19:7; 23:3). The women apply it to both Obed and Ruth: the child will provide for Naomi's old age, and Ruth's hesed toward Naomi is explicitly valued above seven sons — an extraordinary statement in a culture where sons were the highest social value. The seven-son comparison (the ideal family: 1 Sam 2:5; Job 42:13) being exceeded by one faithful foreign daughter-in-law is the book's final inversion of normal social values.
4.16: Naomi takes the child and lays him in her bosom and becomes his nurse (ômenek H546 — same root as the word for a male guardian/tutor, here used for a female caretaker). The image of Naomi nursing Obed is powerful in context: the woman who complained of going out full and returning empty (1:21) is now full — holding the child who embodies YHWH's reversal of her condition. The act of nursing also functionally adopts Obed as Naomi's son, ensuring her care in old age. The covenant community's recognition that 'a son has been born to Naomi' (v17) means the community officially recognizes this adoptive relationship.
4.17: The neighbors' naming of the child — 'They gave him a name, saying, “A son has been born to Naomi”' — is an unusual feature: normally the father or mother names the child. The community's act of naming suggests that this child belongs to the whole community and not only to his parents; his birth is a communal event because it is the completion of the book's covenant purpose. Obed (עֹובֵד) means 'servant' or 'worshiper' — the one who serves and worships YHWH. 'He was the father of Jesse, the father of David' — the genealogical climax the book has been building toward. Ruth the Moabite is David's great-grandmother, and through David, in the messianic genealogy, an ancestor of the Christ.
4.18: The closing genealogy (vv18-22) traces the line from Perez (son of Judah and Tamar, Gen 38) to David — ten generations spanning approximately 400-500 years (from the patriarchal period to the early monarchy). The genealogy echoes the structure of the Gen 5 and Gen 11 genealogies (ten generations from Adam to Noah; ten from Shem to Abraham) — it is stylized to signal a divinely ordered sequence. The genealogy functions as a theological claim: the line from Perez through Boaz to David is YHWH's intentional construction, not historical accident. Each link in the chain — including Tamar's unconventional conception and Ruth's Moabite origin — is part of the divine design.
4.19: Hezron, Ram, Amminadab — the middle generations of the Perez-to-David chain. These names appear in the tribal genealogies of Judah (1 Chr 2:5-15) and in both of the NT Davidic genealogies (Matt 1:3-4; Luke 3:32-33). Amminadab is the father-in-law of Aaron (Exod 6:23), placing this generation at the time of the exodus. The genealogy thus provides a chronological framework: Perez (pre-Egyptian sojourn) → Amminadab (exodus generation) → Nahshon → Salmon → Boaz (early judges period) → Obed → Jesse → David (ca. 1000 BCE). The time-span compression of the genealogy is consistent with other ANE genealogical conventions, which omit intermediate generations.
4.20: Nahshon son of Amminadab is the leader of the tribe of Judah in the wilderness (Num 1:7; 2:3; 7:12); his prominence in the exodus and wilderness period makes him one of the named ancestors of the Davidic line. Salmon is his son, and Matthew 1:5 names Salmon's wife as Rahab — Rahab the Canaanite woman who protected the Israelite spies (Josh 2; 6:22-25). If the Rahab of Joshua is Boaz's mother, then both Boaz's parents were associated with Gentile women who entered the covenant community through exceptional faith and loyalty — a pattern strikingly parallel to Ruth herself. Matthew's genealogy preserves four women (Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, Bathsheba) as the four maternal exceptions in the otherwise male lineage, all with non-standard entry into Israel's covenant story.
4.21: Salmon begot Boaz — closing the loop from Rahab's generation to the protagonist of the book. The genealogy economically presents the entire chain without elaboration, trusting that the book has already told the story. The genealogy's function in Ruth is parallel to Matthew 1: it places an individual story within YHWH's long covenant purpose. Ruth's hesed to Naomi, Boaz's hesed to Ruth and Naomi, and YHWH's hesed in arranging their encounter — all of this serves the genealogical purpose of producing Obed, producing Jesse, producing David. The personal story and the cosmic purpose converge in the genealogy.
4.22: Jesse begot David — the final line, the climactic name. The genealogy's arrival at David is the book's final declaration of purpose. Ruth the Moabite woman, through her covenant loyalty and YHWH's providential arrangement, is the great-grandmother of Israel's greatest king and the ancestor (through David) of the Messiah (Matt 1:1: 'Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham'). The book of Ruth is thus simultaneously a story about hesed in action, a legal case-study in the go'el institution, and a theological explanation of why a Gentile woman's faithfulness became the vehicle for the Davidic line. The Gentile inclusion in the covenant community is not an afterthought of the NT; it begins, structurally and genealogically, in Ruth.
